mockBank.question: A patient undergoing IV midazolam sedation complains of a painful, burning sensation at the injection site. Which ONE of the following explains this if diazepam (Diazemuls) were used instead?
options: A. Diazemuls is more acidic than midazolam — causing burning at physiological pH
B. Diazemuls is dissolved in organic solvent (soya bean oil) which can cause pain on injection and thrombophlebitis
C. Diazemuls contains a higher concentration of benzodiazepine per ml than midazolam
D. Diazemuls is given as a rapid bolus, causing vascular damage
E. Diazemuls has a lower pKa than midazolam — more of the drug enters the vein wall
mockBank.explanation: Diazepam (plain Diazemuls) is insoluble in water — it is dissolved in organic solvents (propylene glycol) or emulsified in soya bean oil (Diazemuls). The organic solvent formulation causes intense venous pain and thrombophlebitis. Midazolam avoids this — it is water-soluble at pH 4 (non-irritant) yet becomes lipid-soluble at physiological pH.

mockBank.question: Hyperalgesia differs from allodynia. Which ONE of the following BEST defines allodynia?
options: A. An increased pain response to a normally painful stimulus
B. Spontaneous pain in the absence of any stimulus
C. Pain in response to a stimulus that is not normally painful
D. Reduced pain threshold from peripheral sensitisation only
E. Pain radiating beyond the site of the original injury
mockBank.explanation: Allodynia = pain from a stimulus that is not normally painful (e.g. light touch causing pain). Hyperalgesia = increased pain from a normally painful stimulus (e.g. mild heat causing severe pain). Both result from sensitisation — peripheral or central. Divergence (radiation) is related but different.

mockBank.question: A patient undergoes an inferior alveolar nerve block. Ten minutes later the lower lip and chin feel numb but the molar tooth remains sensitive. Which ONE of the following is the MOST likely explanation?
options: A. The patient is allergic to the local anaesthetic
B. The nerve block was placed too far anteriorly missing the main trunk, or the mandibular canal is in an unusual position — accessory innervation (mylohyoid nerve) may be present
C. The lidocaine has been inactivated by the inflammatory tissue
D. The maximum dose of LA has been exceeded causing systemic toxicity
E. The patient is a fast metaboliser and the LA has already been inactivated
mockBank.explanation: Numbness of lip/chin (cutaneous distribution of mental nerve) confirms the block was partially effective. Persistent tooth sensitivity suggests: (1) block placed too far anteriorly on the nerve trunk, anaesthetising distal branches first; (2) accessory innervation via the mylohyoid nerve — supplement with a lingual or buccal infiltration; (3) infected/hyperaemic tissue reducing LA efficacy at the apex.

mockBank.question: Which ONE of the following recreational drug interactions with adrenaline in dental LA is of greatest concern?
options: A. Cannabis — reduces metabolism of adrenaline in the liver
B. Cocaine — blocks reuptake of noradrenaline, dramatically potentiating adrenergic effects — risk of hypertensive crisis
C. Heroin — causes direct cardiotoxicity when combined with adrenaline
D. Alcohol — inhibits adrenaline at the receptor level
E. MDMA — reduces adrenaline clearance from the synapse
mockBank.explanation: Cocaine blocks neuronal catecholamine reuptake — when exogenous adrenaline (from LA) is added, there is massive potentiation of sympathomimetic effects: dangerous hypertension, tachycardia, arrhythmia risk. If a patient is known to have used cocaine, wait minimum 24 hours before using adrenaline-containing LA.

mockBank.question: Ibuprofen achieves its analgesic effect by blockage of which ONE of the following enzymes?
options: A. Phospholipase A2 — preventing arachidonic acid release
B. Lipoxygenase — preventing leukotriene synthesis
C. Cyclo-oxygenase (COX-1 and COX-2) — preventing prostaglandin and thromboxane synthesis
D. Monoamine oxidase — preventing breakdown of inflammatory mediators
E. Dihydrofolate reductase — preventing prostaglandin precursor synthesis
mockBank.explanation: NSAIDs (including ibuprofen) non-selectively inhibit both COX-1 and COX-2. COX-1 inhibition → reduced gastric mucosal protection (side effect); COX-2 inhibition → reduced prostaglandin synthesis at sites of inflammation → analgesia and anti-inflammatory effects. The 'stomach ache' side effect is from COX-1 inhibition reducing gastroprotective prostaglandins.
